_note: EDITABLE PLACEHOLDER CONTENT. The five domains and their exam weights are from NVIDIA's official NCA-GENL blueprint (https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/learn/certification/generative-ai-llm-associate/). The individual objective wordings below are representative study targets mapped onto that blueprint, NOT verbatim official sub-objectives. Edit, add, split, or delete these freely — this file is just a starting point. Seeding uses INSERT OR IGNORE keyed on (cert_path, title), so re-running the seed will not duplicate rows.

objectives.title: Transformer architecture fundamentals
objectives.domain: Core Machine Learning and AI Knowledge
objectives.description: Explain self-attention, multi-head attention, positional encoding, and layer normalization, and how they combine into a transformer block.

objectives.title: Neural network training basics
objectives.domain: Core Machine Learning and AI Knowledge
objectives.description: Describe layers, activation functions, loss functions, gradient descent, and backpropagation — how a network actually learns.

objectives.title: Model architecture families
objectives.domain: Core Machine Learning and AI Knowledge
objectives.description: Distinguish encoder-only, decoder-only, and encoder-decoder models and pick the right family for a task.

objectives.title: Pre-training, inference, and scaling laws
objectives.domain: Core Machine Learning and AI Knowledge
objectives.description: Understand how LLMs are pre-trained, how inference works, and what scaling laws and foundation-model concepts imply.

objectives.title: Prompt engineering and alignment concepts
objectives.domain: Core Machine Learning and AI Knowledge
objectives.description: Apply zero-shot, few-shot, chain-of-thought, and system prompts; explain alignment methods like RLHF and instruction tuning.

objectives.title: Build LLM apps with Python libraries
objectives.description: Use Python with Hugging Face Transformers and LangChain to build LLM-powered applications.

objectives.title: NVIDIA generative-AI ecosystem
objectives.description: Apply NeMo, NIM microservices, and Triton Inference Server in an LLM workflow.

objectives.title: LLM integration and agentic workflows
objectives.description: Integrate and deploy LLMs into applications, including orchestration and agentic patterns (e.g., LangChain/LangGraph).

objectives.title: Software engineering best practices for LLM systems
objectives.description: Apply modular code, version control, and clean API design to production LLM systems.

objectives.title: Inference optimization and serving
objectives.description: Understand quantization, batching, and other serving concepts that make LLM deployment efficient.

objectives.title: Fine-tuning and parameter-efficient adaptation
objectives.description: Apply full fine-tuning and PEFT methods including LoRA and QLoRA, and know when each is appropriate.

objectives.title: Evaluation metrics for LLMs
objectives.description: Select and interpret perplexity, BLEU, ROUGE, and BERTScore for the right kind of task.

objectives.title: Experiment design and model comparison
objectives.description: Design experiments and use A/B testing and benchmarking to compare models fairly.

objectives.title: LLM-as-a-judge and automated evaluation
objectives.description: Use LLM-as-a-judge and automated evaluation harnesses to assess model quality at scale.

objectives.title: Reproducibility and validation
objectives.description: Validate model behavior and reproducibility across experimental trials.

objectives.title: Text data cleaning and feature engineering
objectives.description: Perform data cleaning, preprocessing pipelines, and feature engineering for text data.

objectives.title: Tokenization and embeddings
objectives.description: Apply BPE, WordPiece, and SentencePiece tokenization and generate text embeddings / vector representations.

objectives.title: Interpreting statistical metrics
objectives.description: Interpret metrics such as loss and explained-variance ratio to guide model-development decisions.

objectives.title: Data visualization for insight
objectives.description: Create and read visualizations that communicate dataset insights clearly.

objectives.title: GPU-accelerated data processing
objectives.description: Use GPU-accelerated tooling (e.g., RAPIDS / cuDF) for large-scale data processing where appropriate.

objectives.title: Bias detection and mitigation
objectives.description: Detect and minimize bias using fairness metrics and mitigation strategies.

objectives.title: Content filtering and hallucination mitigation
objectives.description: Apply content filtering and techniques that prevent or reduce hallucinated output.

objectives.title: Privacy, security, and consent
objectives.description: Balance data privacy, security, and user consent in LLM applications.

objectives.title: Responsible and ethical AI practices
objectives.description: Follow responsible/ethical AI principles and governance practices.

objectives.title: Safety and alignment risks in deployment
objectives.description: Recognize safety and alignment risks in deployed generative-AI systems.

questions.question_text: In one or two sentences, explain what self-attention computes and why it lets a transformer model long-range dependencies.
questions.expected_answer: Self-attention computes a weighted sum of all token representations, where the weights come from query-key similarity. Because every token can attend directly to every other token, long-range dependencies are a single hop away — no recurrence needed.

questions.question_text: Why do transformers need positional encoding?
questions.expected_answer: Self-attention is permutation-invariant and has no built-in notion of order, so positional information must be injected (via sinusoidal or learned positional encodings) for the model to use token order.

questions.question_text: When would you choose an encoder-only model (e.g., BERT) over a decoder-only model (e.g., GPT)?
questions.expected_answer: Encoder-only for understanding/representation tasks needing bidirectional context (classification, embeddings, NER). Decoder-only for autoregressive generation.

questions.question_text: What is chain-of-thought prompting and when does it help?
questions.expected_answer: Prompting the model to produce intermediate reasoning steps before its final answer. It helps on multi-step reasoning, arithmetic, and logic tasks, primarily in larger models.

questions.question_text: Explain the difference between instruction tuning and RLHF.
questions.expected_answer: Instruction tuning is supervised fine-tuning on (instruction, response) pairs so the model follows instructions. RLHF further optimizes the model against a reward model trained on human preference comparisons (typically via PPO) to align outputs with human preferences.

questions.question_text: What problem does LoRA solve, and how?
questions.expected_answer: Full fine-tuning updates all weights (expensive, large checkpoints). LoRA freezes the base weights and learns small low-rank update matrices injected into layers, drastically cutting trainable parameters and memory while keeping quality.

questions.question_text: What does the 'Q' in QLoRA add over plain LoRA?
questions.expected_answer: QLoRA quantizes the frozen base model (e.g., 4-bit NF4) to cut memory further, while training LoRA adapters in higher precision on top — enabling fine-tuning of large models on a single GPU.

questions.question_text: What does perplexity measure, and does lower or higher indicate a better language model?
questions.expected_answer: Perplexity is the exponentiated average negative log-likelihood the model assigns to held-out text. Lower is better — the model is less 'surprised' by the data.

questions.question_text: Contrast BLEU and ROUGE — what task is each typically used for?
questions.expected_answer: BLEU is precision-oriented n-gram overlap, common for machine translation. ROUGE is recall-oriented n-gram / longest-common-subsequence overlap, common for summarization.

questions.question_text: What problem does subword tokenization (BPE/WordPiece) solve compared to word-level tokenization?
questions.expected_answer: It handles rare and unseen words and bounds vocabulary size by splitting words into frequent subword units, avoiding huge vocabularies and out-of-vocabulary tokens.

questions.question_text: How does quantization reduce inference cost, and what is the trade-off?
questions.expected_answer: It represents weights/activations in lower precision (e.g., INT8 or 4-bit), cutting memory and bandwidth and speeding inference. The trade-off is potential accuracy loss if quantization is too aggressive.

questions.question_text: What distinguishes an 'agentic' LLM workflow from a single prompt-response call?
questions.expected_answer: An agent loops: it can plan, call tools/APIs, observe the results, and iterate over multiple steps toward a goal — rather than producing one output from one prompt.

questions.question_text: Name two techniques to reduce hallucinations in an LLM application.
questions.expected_answer: Any two of: retrieval-augmented generation grounded in cited sources; output validation / constrained decoding; lowering temperature; instructing the model to abstain when unsure; post-hoc fact-checking.

questions.question_text: Give one way bias can enter an LLM and one mitigation.
questions.expected_answer: Bias commonly enters via skewed training data or annotation. Mitigations include data curation/balancing, fairness evaluation across groups, debiasing fine-tuning, and output filtering.

labs.title: Effect of temperature on answer determinism
labs.hypothesis: Lowering temperature from 0.7 to 0.0 will make repeated answers to the same factual prompt nearly identical.
labs.what_changed: Sampling temperature only (0.7 -> 0.2 -> 0.0), same prompt and model, 5 generations each.
labs.commands_config:
model=llama-3.1-8b-instruct
prompt="List the three main components of a transformer block."
temperature in {0.7, 0.2, 0.0}; top_p=1.0; n=5
labs.observed_result: At 0.7 wording varied and one run added an extra (wrong) component. At 0.0 all 5 runs were token-identical and correct.
labs.why_it_happened: Greedy decoding at temperature 0 always picks the argmax token, removing sampling variance; higher temperature flattens the distribution so lower-probability (sometimes wrong) tokens get chosen.
labs.what_next: Test whether temperature 0 hurts on a task that benefits from diversity (brainstorming), to feel the trade-off.

labs.title: LoRA vs full fine-tune: trainable params and VRAM
labs.hypothesis: A LoRA adapter (r=8) will train a tiny fraction of parameters and fit in far less VRAM than a full fine-tune of the same model.
labs.what_changed: Adaptation method: full fine-tune vs LoRA (r=8, alpha=16) on the same small instruction dataset.
labs.commands_config:
base=llama-3.1-8b
lora: r=8, alpha=16, target_modules=[q_proj,v_proj]
batch_size=4, epochs=1
labs.observed_result: LoRA reported ~4-5M trainable params vs ~8B for full FT, and fit on a single 16GB GPU where full FT OOM'd.
labs.why_it_happened: LoRA freezes base weights and only learns low-rank update matrices injected into attention projections, so optimizer state (the big VRAM cost) is proportional to the tiny adapter, not the whole model.
labs.what_next: Add QLoRA (4-bit base) and compare VRAM and final eval loss against the r=8 LoRA run.
